coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from waste chicken feathers for chlortetracycline sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Linping
Liu, Jiawen
Long, Xiang
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we present a novel, straightforward, eco-friendly and cost-effective method for synthesizing fluorescent carbon dots. A simple one-pot hydrothermal method was used to create blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from waste chicken feathers and ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) as precursors. The research demonstrates that N-CDs not only exhibit excellent photostability, but also serve as a versatile nanoscale fluorescence sensor for the efficient qualitative and quantitative detection of chlortetracycline (CTC) in samples. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is attributed to the specific and rapid quenching of N-CDs fluorescence by chlortetracycline through the static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE). The concentration of CTC within the range of 0.05–0.35 mM exhibits a significant linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching of NCDs, as indicated by the formula (I0-I)/I = 4.74248c(CTC)-0.09041. Subsequent spiked recovery experiment confirms the feasibility of this method for the detection of CTC in samples. Therefore, leveraging the concept of turning waste into treasure, this study successfully developed a green and cost-effective method for synthesizing NCDs from natural biomass, offering a novel perspective for further development of multifunctional sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibiotic detection
Carbon dots
Feathers
Static quenching
Inner filter effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of N-CDs.

Structural characterization of NCDs. (a) TEM image, particle size distribution (upper right inset) and HRTEM (lower right inset); (b) Infrared spectrum; (c) XRD pattern; (d) XPS survey spectrum; (e–g) XPS fine spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively.

Optical properties of NCDs. (a) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum; (b) Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum (black), excitation spectrum (red), emission spectrum (blue); (c) Normalized PL emission spectra of NCDs at different excitation wavelengths.
Stability measurement of NCDs. Effects of (a) Ionic strength, (b) pH, (c) time, (d) temperature, (e) different solvents on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs.
Optimization of detection conditions. (a) The amount of NCDs; (b) pH; (c) Time.

CTC detection probe. (a) (I 0-I)/I (blue) of NCDs after adding different antibiotics, (I 0-I)/I (orange) of NCDs + CTC solution after adding other antibiotics as interfering substances. (b) Fluorescence response of CTC and NCDs with different concentrations; (c) Linear fitting line of (I 0-I) / I and CTC concentration.

Sensing mechanism (a) UV-visible absorption spectra of CTC, N-CDs and N-CDs + CTC; (b) UV–Vis spectrum of CTC and PL excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs; (c) Fluorescence decay traces of the probe and probe + CTC.
Comparison with previously reported probes in the literature.
Experimental results of CTC spiked recovery.
Comparison with the previous methods for detecting CTC.

row.entry: High sensitivity, low cost, cumbersome operation, many interference factors
High sensitivity, high cost, complex sample preparation process
Simple operation, requires professional operators and high technical content
Fast analysis speed, long sample processing time
High sensitivity, fast analysis speed, poor selectivity

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from waste chicken feathers for chlortetracycline sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we present a novel, straightforward, eco-friendly and cost-effective method for synthesizing fluorescent carbon dots. A simple one-pot hydrothermal method was used to create blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) from waste chicken feathers and ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) as precursors. The research demonstrates that N-CDs not only exhibit excellent photostability, but also serve as a versatile nanoscale fluorescence sensor for the efficient qualitative and quantitative detection of chlortetracycline (CTC) in samples. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is attributed to the specific and rapid quenching of N-CDs fluorescence by chlortetracycline through the static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE). The concentration of CTC within the range of 0.05–0.35 mM exhibits a significant linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching of NCDs, as indicated by the formula (I0-I)/I = 4.74248c(CTC)-0.09041. Subsequent spiked recovery experiment confirms the feasibility of this method for the detection of CTC in samples. Therefore, leveraging the concept of turning waste into treasure, this study successfully developed a green and cost-effective method for synthesizing NCDs from natural biomass, offering a novel perspective for further development of multifunctional sensors.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlortetracycline (CTC) is a highly effective and broad-spectrum antibiotic, extensively used in animal husbandry and aquaculture to prevent and treat animal diseases, as well as to enhance livestock growth rates [1,2]. Owing to its antimicrobial properties, CTC is not fully absorbed by livestock and poultry. Consequently, residual CTC can contaminate surface water through livestock excretions and urine [3]. Furthermore, residues of CTC in animals may enter the human food chain, posing a potential threat to human health [4,5]. Qiu et al.'s research indicates that CTC can be passed from one generation to the next, diminishing the immune defense capabilities of offspring and raising concerns about its harm to natural populations [6]. Therefore, accurate and rapid detection of CTC residues is crucial for ensuring food safety and maintaining public health [7]. Currently, various methods are available for detecting CTC, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], and thin-layer chromatography (TLC) [10]. While most methods demonstrate satisfactory sensitivity, some are limited by complex instrumentation, lengthy procedures, specialized expertise, and poor reproducibility, thereby restricting their practical applications.

In comparison, fluorescent detection probes, serving as a powerful tool, have been extensively applied in analysis due to their simplicity, non-destructive nature, and high sensitivity [11,12]. Carbon dots (CDs) are defined as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm [13]. Their surfaces are enriched with functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, or aromatic rings, which endow them with outstanding water solubility [14,15]. The conjugated carbon framework enhances the stability of the carbon dots. Since the discovery of single-walled carbon nanotubes made by Xu et al. in 2004 using the arc discharge technique [16], carbon dots have become a prominent member of the fluorescent carbon nanomaterial family and have attracted considerable attention. Compared to conventional rare-earth fluorescein and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots exhibit distinctive advantages [17,18], including low toxicity, exceptional water solubility, tunable fluorescence properties, and ease of functionalization, making them an ideal substitute for traditional semiconductor quantum dots [19]. Additionally, due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, outstanding optical properties, and cost-effectiveness, carbon dots (CDs) hold broad application prospects in high-definition displays [20], biological imaging [21], energy conversion [22], medical detection [23], and various other fields. Currently, there have been advancements in the utilization of fluorescence-based probes for the surveillance of clinical antibiotic diagnostics. For instance, Sara et al. have used the conventional antibiotic metronidazole (MET) in conjunction with biocompatible carbon quantum dots to track and quantify drug presence within cells [24]; Yadav et al. developed mesoporous matrices and luminescent labels composed of carbon for the identification and capture of specific nitrofuran antibiotics, among others [25]. Nevertheless, some of these probes are often affected by complex preparation processes, high costs, and time-consuming detection. Therefore, the development of simple, low-cost, excellent luminescent performance, and environmentally friendly nanoprobes is a necessary condition for efficient detection of CTC.

Waste chicken feathers are low-cost and readily available biomass resources [26,27]. Each year, a significant quantity of feathers from poultry processing plants are discarded, yet their utilization in the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots effectively reuses these resources. Feathers are primarily composed of proteins (primarily keratin) and several other large biological molecules [28,29]. During high-temperature heating, these biological molecules break down to produce precursor materials rich in carbon, serving as a carbon source for the formation of carbon dots. Feathers also contain minor heteroatoms such as sulfur [30], which can be incorporated into the carbon dot structures during high-temperature processing, endowing the dots with excellent optical properties and biocompatibility. The preparation of fluorescent carbon dots from discarded feathers not only facilitates resource recycling but also is simple and economical. The resulting carbon dots exhibit favorable biocompatibility and low toxicity, holding potential applications in fields such as biological imaging and detection. The abundance of feather sources and the eco-friendly preparation method adhere to contemporary environmental conservation standards, ensuring sustainability in the process.

This study utilizes feathers as the carbon source, combined with a nitrogen doping strategy employing ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) as the nitrogen source. Hydrothermal synthesis was employed to produce biomass-derived NCDs. The morphology, structure, and optical properties of the NCDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy, demonstrating outstanding optical performance and biocompatibility. Research indicates that the static quenching and inner filter effect between CTC and NCDs quench the fluorescence of NCDs. Consequently, a sensitive and rapid method for detecting CTC has been developed with a detection limit of 0.17 μ M. This method offers excellent selectivity and strong resistance to interference, making it suitable for the efficient analysis of CTC in practical sample settings. The sensor is made from sustainable materials and possesses advanced detection capabilities. It holds significant application value in food safety and drug analysis, offering broad prospects for the efficient and accurate detection of antibiotics in the future.

Chlortetracycline (CTC), gentamicin sulfate (GSS), amoxicillin (AM), ampicillin (AMP), and other antibiotics were from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) was from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. Acetonitrile (ACN), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), methanol (MeOH), isopropanol (IPA), and ethanol (EtOH) were from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate (REF) was from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All chemicals are AR grade and can be used directly without further purification. Distilled water was self-made secondary distilled water. Waste chicken feathers were collected from a local vegetable market.

The 25 mL hydrothermal reaction autoclave was purchased from Xiniukeji Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaner was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. The electric constant temperature air-drying oven was purchased from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–Vis spectra were measured using a Shunyu Hengping 756 PC UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer. FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TEIN transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for the morphological study. Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR) was used to determine infrared spectrum. Using BrukerD8 ADVANCE X-ray diffraction (XRD) for crystal structure analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was conducted on K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), Thermo, USA. Fluorescence lifetimes were captured by an Edinburgh FFLS-920 spectrofluorometer (Livingstone, UK).

NCDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method, utilizing discarded feathers as the carbon source and NH4Cl as the nitrogen source. The feathers were first washed with distilled water, sun-dried, and then crushed into powder using a juicer for later use. Subsequently, 0.1 g of feathers were placed into a 50 mL beaker. The mixture was then sequentially added with 10 mL of distilled water and 0.27 g (5 mmol) of NH4Cl, followed by thorough mixing using an ultrasonic cleaner for 10 min. The mixture was then transferred to a 25 mL hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling naturally to ambient temperature, the NCDs solution was first filtered using filter paper to remove large particle impurities, then dialyzed against a dialysis membrane (500-1000 Da) for 12 h to eliminate other small molecules, ultimately yielding blue NCDs (Fig. 1 ).

In order to investigate the stability of carbon dots in different environments, we added a certain amount of NCDs solution, measured the resistance of carbon dots to ionic strength when the salt solution was 0-1000 mM. Then we used a strong HCl acid solution to adjust the pH to 1–6, and a strong NaOH alkali solution to regulate the pH to 8–13, and measured the acid and alkali resistance of carbon dots in the pH range of 1–13, measured the time stability of carbon dots every other day from Day 1 to Day 7, measured the temperature stability of carbon dots every 10 °C in the range of 20 °C–70 °C, and measured the effects of seven common solvents (such as EtOH, DMF, THF, etc.) on the luminescence properties of carbon dots.

A total of 100 μL of NCDs solution was taken and added with similar antibiotics, such as oxytetracycline, amoxicillin, gentamicin sulfate, etc., and its fluorescence intensity was then measured under optimal conditions. Then, 100 μL of NCDs solution was added with chlortetracycline and the aforementioned interfering substances were added respectively. The fluorescence intensity was measured under the same conditions to investigate the effect of interfering substances on the fluorescence of NCDs in the presence of chlortetracycline alone and in the presence of chlortetracycline.

The structure and chemical composition of NCDs were analyzed using TEM, FTIR, XRD, and XPS. The morphology of NCDs was observed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the NCDs particles exhibit an irregular granular distribution with an average size of approximately 3.8 nm. HRTEM (inset) reveals a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the graphene (100) crystal plane. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 2b) indicates that the absorption band near 3438 cm-1 is attributed to OH or NH stretching vibrations; the moderate absorption peak at 1636 cm-1 corresponds to -C=N stretching vibrations; the bimodal intensity at 1407 cm-1 suggests a 1:1 ratio, indicating a correlation with -C(CH3)2. The weaker peak at 993 cm-1 corresponds to the CN stretching vibration peak, while the tensile peak at 621 cm-1 appears to be related to the out-of-plane bending vibration of CH. In summary, the synthesized NCDs contain abundant functional groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups on their surface, while also preserving the long carbon chain structure inherent to feathers. X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a commonly used technique for analyzing the structure and crystal structure of carbon quantum dots. The detection results are shown in Fig. 2c, with a characteristic peak at 2θ=23.38° attributed to the (002) crystal plane with a lattice spacing of 0.34–0.36 nm. This peak indicates that our synthesized material possesses the fundamental properties of carbon dots. Additionally, the absence of broad peaks suggests a correlation with the small particle size and crystalline structure of NCDs. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2d) indicates that NCDs are primarily composed of C, O, and N, with respective contents of 61.73 %, 18.59 %, and 16.29 %, and a few Cl and S, confirming the successful synthesis of N-doped CDs. The chemical composition of the NCDs surface was further examined using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The XPS spectrum revealed three main peaks: C1s, N1s, and O1s. As depicted in Fig. 2e, high-resolution C1s can be resolved into three peaks: C-C (284.78 eV), C-N (285.98 eV), and C=O (288.21 eV). The two peaks in high-resolution N1s (Fig. 2f) can be resolved into N-H (399.91 eV) and N-C (401.41 eV). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) features two peaks at 531.49 eV and 532.61 eV, corresponding to O=C and O-C, respectively. The XPS spectroscopy findings are consistent with those from FTIR analysis.

We examined the optical characteristics of NCDs, including the FL spectrum, UV–visible absorption spectrum, and photostability. The three-dimensional scanning image of the FL of carbon dots is depicted in Fig. 3 a, showing a main peak and a secondary peak, with the presence of minor secondary peaks indicating that NCDs possess the fundamental traits of carbon dots. The FL spectrum and UV-visible absorption spectrum of NCDs are depicted in Fig. 3b. A distinct absorption peak emerges near 240 nm, potentially due to the π→π* transition of C=C [31]. The transparent NCDs solution exhibits a strong blue FL, with a quantum yield (QY) of 15.42 % in aqueous solution, calculated using quinine sulfate (REF) as a reference. The maximum emission peak of NCDs is situated at 445 nm. The normalized graph in Fig. 3c demonstrates that as the excitation wavelength shifts from 350 nm to 400 nm, both the intensity and position of the fluorescence emission peak for NCDs undergo significant changes, shifting by 29 nm to the red, indicating that NCDs exhibit excitation-wavelength-dependent characteristics, potentially due to quantum effects and uneven particle sizes.

As fluorescent CDs, it is essential to analyze their fluorescence stability. NaCl, a well-known strong electrolyte, can be fully ionized into Na+ and Cl-. Given its widespread distribution of cations and anions, it is often used as a representative for ion strength experiments. Consequently, this study employs NaCl to adjust the ion strength. As the NaCl concentration increased from 0 to 1000 mM, there was almost no change in the FL intensity (Fig. 4 a), indicating that the NCDs exhibit excellent ion strength tolerance and are unaffected by it, suggesting significant potential for use in physiological salt environments. Fig. 4b illustrates the impact of pH on the fluorescence stability of NCDs. The fluorescence of NCDs remains relatively stable across pH levels 3–11. However, at pH levels 1–2 and 12–13, the fluorescence intensity decreases, indicating that NCDs only exhibit strong quenching in strong acid and strong base conditions, demonstrating their suitability across a wide pH range. Fig. 4c illustrates the fluorescence intensity measurements of NCDs over a one-week period, showing no notable changes. Even after a month of storage, no suspended particles were observed in the NCDs solution, and their fluorescence intensity remained essentially unchanged, demonstrating excellent stability over time. Fig. 4d presents the temperature stability results of the NCDs. A slight but insignificant decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed when the temperature was raised from 20 °C to 70 °C, suggesting that NCDs are capable of maintaining their fluorescence performance even under elevated temperatures. Given the diverse applications of carbon dots, the PL emission spectra of NCDs were also examined in seven common solvents: acetonitrile (ACN), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), methanol (MeOH), water (WAT), ethanol (EtOH), and isopropanol (IPA). As shown in Fig. 4e, no significant changes were observed in the strongest emission peak position or emission pattern. In conclusion, the synthesized NCDs demonstrate excellent fluorescence stability.

Experiments demonstrate that NCDs exhibit robust fluorescence stability, and CTC notably quenches the fluorescence of NCDs, laying the groundwork for their use as fluorescent probes for sensitive CTC detection. To optimize the detection conditions—the amount of NCDs, solution pH, and duration of the reaction—we conducted experiments. I0 denotes the fluorescence intensity of NCDs before adding CTC (0.1 mM), while I represents the fluorescence intensity after adding CTC. The optimization results are depicted in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a denotes that the amount of NCDs significantly influences the detection outcomes of CTC. As the amount increases, the difference between I 0 and I initially increases and then decreases, reaching its maximum (i.e. I minimum) at NCDs=100 μL. This suggests that for a given molar concentration of CTC, lower amounts of NCDs yield superior sensitivity denotes, in subsequent experiments, we will consistently use 100 μL of NCDs. The impact of solution pH on detection outcomes is depicted in Fig. 5b. As the solution transitions from acidic to alkaline, there is no discernible trend in I 0-I values. Nevertheless, it appears that alkaline conditions are superior to acidic ones, with the difference peaking at pH=11. For biological applications, the subsequent experiments will be conducted at pH=8. Fig. 5c illustrates the effect of reaction time on detection efficiency. Following the addition of CTC, no significant changes were observed in the I 0-I values over time. Consequently, rapid detection of CTC is feasible, and subsequent experiments can proceed with direct measurements following the introduction of CTC.

To specifically detect CTC, NCDs fluorescent probes must demonstrate superior selectivity. In this study, 18 common antibiotics were used as interferents, including glycine (GLY), hydroxyproline (HYP), streptomycin (SM), ascorbic acid (VC), threonine (THR), glutamic acid (GLU), oxytetracycline (OTC), lysine (LYS), histidine (HIS), erythromycin (EM), phenylalanine (PHE), ceftazidime (CAE), chloramphenicol (CP), kanamycin sulfate (KANA), ampicillin (AMP), tetracycline (TC), amoxicillin (AM), and gentamicin sulfate (GSS). We studied the selectivity of chlortetracycline (CTC) for NCDs. In the experiment, 2 mL of an aqueous solution and 100 μL of the NCDs stock solution (with a fluorescence intensity of I 0) were added to a quartz cuvette. CTC and 18 interfering substances were then added, with the fluorescence intensity of NCDs recorded as I. The effect of antibiotics on the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was measured, and the (I0-I)/I values were calculated, as depicted by the blue band in Fig. 6 a. Different antibiotics exhibit varying degrees of fluorescence quenching on NCDs, with CTC notably quenching the fluorescence. Furthermore, based this observation, we measured the fluorescence response of the probe when 18 types of interferences coexisted with CTC, calculating the (I0-I)/I values, as depicted by the orange band in Fig. 6a. It is evident that the presence of interferences did not significantly affect the quenching effect of CTC. The above findings demonstrate that the fluorescent probe exhibits excellent selectivity for the detection of CTC.

Sensitivity is a critical factor in evaluating the effectiveness of fluorescent probes. As depicted in Fig. 6b, as the CTC concentration (0.05–0.35 mmol/L) increases, the fluorescence peak intensity of NCDs systematically decreases, with a quenching level of 70 % at 0.35 mM. Fig. 6c presents a linear equation derived from the fluorescence intensity curve of NCDs. The data reveals a strong linear correlation between CTC concentration and NCDs fluorescence intensity within the range of 0.05–0.35 mM CTC concentration. The linear relationship is expressed as (I0-I)/I = 4.74248c(CTC)−0.09041, with a correlation coefficient of R 2=0.9946. Furthermore, using the formula LOD=3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank NCDs and k denotes the slope of the standard curve, the detection limit (LOD) of NCDs is calculated to be 0.17 μM.

Then we compared the NCDs probe with the previously reported probes, as shown in Table 1 . Obviously, these probes have certain limitations in linear range and LOD. In contrast, the NCDs probe has a wider linear range and lower detection limit for CTC detection, which indicates the unique advantages of the NCDs probe.

Furthermore, we employed the standard addition method to evaluate the practical performance of the prepared NCDs fluorescent probe for the determination of CTC. A series of solutions containing known concentrations of CTC were spiked into water and mixed with the NCDs. The results presented in Table 2 reveal that the recovery rates for CTC were exceptionally high, ranging from 92.94 % to 105.64 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) between 1.93 % and 5.64 % (n = 6), indicating high accuracy of the assay. Therefore, this probe demonstrated outstanding potential for CTC sensing in the target samples.

Table 3 compares the proposed method for detecting CTC with the previous methods, as reported in the literature. It is evident that although these methods have high sensitivity and accuracy, they still have shortcomings, such as the need for precision instruments, the complex preparation process of samples, and high cost, which limits their application in some fields. Hence, it is of great significance to develop a simple, low-cost, highly selective and sensitive method to detect CTC. Herein, we used waste chicken feathers as precursors to synthesize carbon dots, which reduced the experimental costs under the premise of ensuring high selectivity and high sensitivity for CTC detection.

Typically, the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots can be categorized as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching, and static quenching [39]. To investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, we examined the UV-visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of NCDs, CTC, and NCDs + CTC. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, new absorption peaks appeared at 274 nm and 371 nm, indicating that new compounds were formed after the combination of CTC and the probe, which may have the static quenching. Generally, when the UV-visible absorption spectrum of a quencher overlaps with the excitation spectrum of a fluorophore, it results in an inner filter effect (IFE) [40], as illustrated in Fig. 7b. CTC exhibits a broad absorption band in the UV-visible range, with a notable overlap between the excitation spectrum of NCDs and the UV-visible absorption spectrum of CTC. Additionally, electron transfer can be induced by the overlap of UV absorption bands. The overlap depicted in Fig. 7a between the UV-visible absorption spectra of CTC and NCDs facilitates charge transfer from the conduction band of NCDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of CTC, resulting in fluorescence quenching [41].

Then we conducted experiments on fluorescence lifetime, as depicted in Fig. 7c. The fluorescence lifetime of the NCDs probe did not change significantly after the addition of CTC, suggesting that the sensing mechanism is a result of static quenching and IFE [42,43].

In summary, based on the concept of economic and environmental protection, this work screened numerous raw materials for experiments in the early stages, and ultimately selected feathers as the carbon source and ammonium chloride as the nitrogen source. Through a simple and inexpensive hydrothermal method, blue NCDs with good luminescence performance and stable structure were synthesized. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the NCDs are 368 nm and 445 nm, respectively. Structural characterization indicates that the carbon dots have successfully doped with N element, and their surface contains functional groups such as -NH2, C=N, -OH, etc. The abundant functional groups and stable optical properties on the surface also lay a solid foundation for the practical application of the carbon dots. Subsequently, based on the static quenching and inner filter effect between NCDs and CTC, we successfully developed a novel and efficient detection method for simple and rapid identification and determination of CTC, with a detection limit of 0.17 μM. Using the standard addition method, this approach has demonstrated significant practical potential and broad application prospects in the analysis and application of samples, thereby opening up new ideas for the effective analysis of CTC in environmental samples. In addition, this research transforms "waste" - feathers into highly valuable fluorescent carbon dots, greatly expanding the selection of raw materials for carbon dots synthesis and providing a clear direction for the large-scale development of new economic and green synthesis techniques in the future.

Linping Ma: Writing – original draft, Validation, Funding acquisition, Investigation. Jiawen Liu: Validation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiang Long: Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.
